text:
# Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Retail](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/index.html)

24/11/2025 • 7 minutes reading

[Henk Hofstede](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/henk-hofstede/index.html)

The popularity of Black Friday and Cyber Monday is stagnating and retailers are starting sales promotions earlier and earlier. The two days of action remain as popular among younger generations and people with financial worries, but older people are increasingly dropping out. At the same time, criticism of excessive consumption continues. Retailers face the challenge of responding to this with targeted strategies.

## Design of the study

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
## Design of the study

As in previous years, the survey was conducted in collaboration with Q&A Retail among 2,000 respondents, divided into five age categories: Generation Z (18 to 25 years), Millennials (26 to 39 years), Generation X (40 to 54 years), Baby Boomers (55 to 69 years) and the Silent Generation (70 to 81 years). Income differences have also been included, ranging from respondents with (much) less to (much) more income than the Dutch average.

## Evolution of Black Friday over time

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
## Evolution of Black Friday over time

It is striking that retailers are increasingly betting on bargains and special promotions well before Black Friday. Black Friday originated as a one-day shopping festival in the United States on the day after Thanksgiving, the fourth Thursday in November. However, some retailers are now taking advantage of Black Friday from November 11, partly as a result of Singles Day, which came from China, the day on which singles can treat themselves. Another reason for the early start of the promotional period is to spread the crowds, because otherwise parcel deliverers would not be able to cope during the busy end of the year. Finally, many retailers also feel pressure to participate because of the competition that is also doing it. The result is that the purchasing period has been considerably extended in the last two months of the year and now covers more than six weeks, from early November to Christmas.

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
The distribution of promotional days is clearly reflected in consumer spending. iDEAL processed fewer online payments for the first time during Black Friday last year; on this day there were 5.8 million transactions, a decrease of no less than 18.4 percent compared to 2023. The number of debit card payments at the cash register also decreased on this day: in 2024, people withdrew their debit card 18.9 million times on Black Friday and used them to pay 558 million euros. In 2023, that number and amount were 20.3 million and 604 million euros respectively, according to the Dutch Payments Association.

However, the number of online payments made by iDEAL in the entire month of November last year increased by 7.5 percent to 51.2 million. The increase over the entire month of November shows that the desire to buy extends over a longer period and that consumers start spending money much earlier than just on the Black Friday weekend, including the following Cyber ​​Monday.

## Stagnation in popularity

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
## Stagnation in popularity

The research by ABN AMRO and Q&A Retail confirms that the popularity of Black Friday and Cyber Monday is stagnating. Before 2025, 60 percent of consumers ('sure' and 'think so') expect to make a purchase. This hardly differs from the 59 percent of respondents who said they expected this in 2024. Our research shows that 47 percent actually made a purchase last year.

It is striking that young people made the most purchases during these promotional days. Generation Z was the most active at 80 percent, while only 15 percent of Baby Boomers and 8 percent of the Silent Generation indicated that they had purchased something last year. Men (52 percent) also bought more on average than women (43 percent) last year. This trend seems to be repeating itself this year: the younger the respondent, the more often they expect to buy something. This is 91 percent for Generation Z and 19 percent for the oldest group.

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
The perception surrounding these action days remains virtually the same compared to last year: the percentage of respondents who do not like the action days (at all) has increased by 1 percent to 33 percent. This increase is at the expense of the percentage that thinks the action days are great (31 percent), which has actually fallen by 1 percentage point. The rest of the respondents are neutral on this. The differences in preferences according to age are large: within the two youngest age groups, more than half find these days (completely) wonderful. In the two oldest groups this is only 8 and 4 percent.

## Other perception and criticism

Just like last year, almost half of respondents (48 percent) believe that Black Friday leads to overconsumption and has a negative impact on the environment and society. This sentiment is especially strong among the two oldest generations. Two-thirds of Baby Boomers and three-quarters of the Silent Generation agree. Nevertheless, 39 percent of respondents see these days as an opportunity to make purchases that they otherwise could not afford. More than half of the two youngest generations and financially (very) vulnerable consumers experience this this way.

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
The main reason for respondents not participating in the promotional days is that they only want to make purchases when they need certain products, rather than being driven by promotions. This outcome is the same as last year. In addition, almost half of the non-participants again mention that the sale days are a nonsensical and commercial promotion.

## Buying behavior and combination with holidays

Of respondents who purchased products during Black Friday last year, more than a third expect to spend (much) more this year. Just over half expect to spend the same amount as in 2024. The wallet plays a major role in purchasing behavior during the promotional days. Of the respondents who find it difficult or impossible to make ends meet, more than 69 and 83 percent respectively, say they use Black Friday and Cyber ​​Monday to purchase luxury products at a discount. That is well above average.

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
46 percent also consciously postpone purchases until these days that they have wanted to make for some time, with the extra discounts as the reason. Last year this was still 50 percent. To the statement 'When I see good offers during Black Friday and Cyber ​​Monday, I am tempted to buy them, even if I did not plan to buy these products beforehand', 43 percent of respondents indicate that they (completely) agree with this. This percentage is slightly higher than last year. It is mainly the three youngest age groups that indicate this and therefore appear to make impulsive purchases more often.

Of the consumers who expect to buy something on Black Friday and Cyber ​​Monday (60 percent), 77 percent use these promotional days to buy gifts for Sinterklaas, Christmas or both holidays. Younger generations say they do this more often than older generations.

## Popular product categories and retailers

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
## Popular product categories and retailers

Clothing and shoes remain the most popular category at 53 percent, followed by electronics at 47 percent. Gift items (38 percent) and drugstore and perfumery products (33 percent) follow in third and fourth place, just like last year. Men more often prefer electronics (59 percent), while women find clothing and shoes (also 59 percent) more important.

In addition, it appears that platform bol scores highest at 32 percent when asked which store or webshop consumers think of when they want to buy something on Black Friday. The platform scores highest among both women and men, regardless of their age. Mediamarkt is mentioned as the second retailer with 17 percent and Amazon in third with 7 percent.

## Black Friday lessons for retailers

As mentioned, the popularity of Black Friday seems to be stagnating, with Generation Z and Millennials remaining the biggest drivers of the shopping festival. In addition, it is an interesting period for financially vulnerable consumers to purchase items. Retailers can respond to this with their marketing strategies by focusing on attractive offers in popular categories such as clothing, shoes, electronics and gift items. The use of online channels and social media offers additional opportunities to reach these target groups.

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
In addition, it is interesting to respond to the increasingly longer purchasing period that starts with Singles Day and ends around Christmas. This offers opportunities to spread typical Black Friday offers over time, use them strategically and rename them. This can be done by linking the promotions in addition to Black Friday more explicitly to Sinterklaas and Christmas.

Finally, there is also strong criticism of overconsumption and the environmental burden that this promotional period entails. Entrepreneurs who specifically do not participate in Black Friday and emphasize their social responsibility can create a positive brand experience. Dille & Kamille's Green Friday is still a good example of this. This year, their customer service on Black Friday will even be renamed 'Plant Service' for customers who do not have green fingers. With fun promotions like these, entrepreneurs distinguish themselves during the great purchasing boom.

## Read more in the retail sector

metadata.source_title: Black Friday popularity is stagnating, sales days are more widespread

text:
## Read more in the retail sector

Today's store is more than just a pantry. It offers customers advice and inspiration and is the place to be in contact with customers. Due to the Ukraine crisis and significant inflation, consumer confidence has fallen sharply and this affects consumer behavior. Retailers are also faced with significant cost increases in the areas of purchasing, rent, personnel costs, Covid recovery payments and energy. Circumstances remain challenging for many retailers even after Covid.
